title: Use distinct button styles for primary and secondary actions
summary: AI-generated interfaces use the same button style for all actions, making it impossible to identify the recommended action.
instruction: Define at least two distinct button visual levels: primary (filled, high contrast) and secondary (outlined, lower contrast). Reserve primary for the most important action per section.
rationale: When every button uses the same style, users cannot distinguish the recommended next step. Button hierarchy is the visual equivalent of a CTA gradient. Flat hierarchies reduce conversion and increase decision time.

violations: A marketing section with 'Sign Up Free', 'Learn More', 'Watch Demo' all using identical filled buttons.
A settings page with 'Save Changes', 'Reset', 'Cancel' all using the same style.
controls: A CTA section with one filled primary ('Start Free Trial') and two outline secondary links.
A settings footer with filled 'Save Changes', outlined 'Reset', plain text 'Cancel'.
borderline: A page with a single action (single CTA) where one button style suffices.

exceptions.description: Toolbars where all buttons are icon-only with equal importance. Command palettes with text-only action lists. Navigation menus with equal-weight links.

detector.signalDescription: Group buttons by section proximity. Compare computed styles. If 3+ buttons share identical styling and one should be logically primary, flag.
limitations: Cannot determine the logical primary action without semantic understanding.
Some button groups have genuinely equal-weight actions.

scenarios: A toolbar where all buttons are equally weighted.
A pricing page with three equally styled 'Choose Plan' buttons.
mitigations: Check for section context. Flag only if the section has one primary goal AND multiple equally styled buttons.

fixturePlan.description: A hero section with all buttons styled as filled primary.
Same hero with filled primary, outline secondary, text tertiary.
A pricing page with three equally styled 'Choose Plan' buttons where each plan is independently promoted.
